reasoning:
The most recent available data for the ICE BofA AA US Corporate Index yield shows it sitting at 4.86% as of March 14, 2025. This reflects a recent run-up from around 4.71% in early March, placing the yield at the upper end of its recent local range. Short-term mean reversion and the FRED statistical trend model (which forecasts a slight downward trajectory and gives a low linear estimate of P>4.86) suggest that the yield is more likely to fall than rise by the first resolution date (March 23). Thus, the initial probability is set at 0.38. 

Over the medium-to-longer term (spanning later in 2025 and into 2026), macroeconomic consensus highlighted in recent market outlooks suggests that corporate bond yields and the 10-year Treasury will likely fall modestly or remain range-bound as the Federal Reserve continues to gradually cut interest rates. This downward structural pressure implies that yields are less likely to stay permanently above the March local peak. However, due to the inherent volatility of bond markets and the strict forecasting directive that uncertainty must increase with the forecast horizon and curve toward 0.5, the probabilities systematically step up toward 0.49 for the 1-year resolution date.
